questions.main_text:
**Retail Trends from the 2012 Holiday Shopping Season**

Even though it caused an [SELECT id=22] among certain consumers, retailers have concluded that the formula for increasing seasonal sales totals is mostly based on increasing holiday promotions, deals, and store hours – hours that now include Thanksgiving night. Not only [SELECT id=23] standard to open by 8 p.m. or 9 p.m. on Thanksgiving day, but the holiday season now basically [SELECT id=24] the backto-school shopping period, with promos and Christmas displays [SELECT id=25] in early September.

​

As expected, this has been [SELECT id=26] very strong season for e-commerce. Online spending was up sharply on Thanksgiving, Black Friday and Cyber Monday [SELECT id=27], and consumers hardly tired of online shopping in the weeks that followed. [SELECT id=28], spending in physical stores was down nearly 2% on Black Friday, and after several sluggish weeks of sales, some experts [SELECT id=29] for holiday spending during the 2012 season. If [SELECT id=30] one dominant trend in retail lately, it’s the utter blurring of online and offline shopping. For quite a while, shoppers have viewed the two modes of [SELECT id=31] as basically interchangeable. In the past, it was almost as if people running a retailer’s website had no contact with the people running the actual stores, leading to confused and frustrated shoppers. Early on, a retailer’s presence in social media might have come [SELECT id=32] of a few ambitious young interns, if there was a presence at all. Today, on the other hand, retailers and analysts talk nonstop about the need to combine online and offline sales efforts, using phrases like “multiscreen shopper” and “omnichannel shopping”. It seems that retailers have [SELECT id=33] to embrace the “omnichannel” shopper by actively trying to lure shoppers into brick-andmortar stores with deals and promotions and by simultaneously ramping up efforts to draw consumers to their websites.

​

What’s more, consumers now often demand free shipping in order to consider an online purchase. [SELECT id=34], free shipping has become almost universal during the holidays, and throughout the year really. [SELECT id=35] the needs of shoppers, retailers provided free shipping as late as two days prior to Christmas Eve with [SELECT id=36] by Christmas Eve. The message being sent to shoppers is this: Any way you want to purchase, we’re going to help you.

​

But there’s nothing new under the sun. Not long ago, layaway had [SELECT id=37] disappeared from the retail scene. Then the Great Recession hit, shoppers grew sick of credit card debts, and the idea of paying off holiday purchases in cash, in fixed installments, sounded terrific – even with all the extra hassle included. To draw shoppers on especially tight budgets, retailers have even lowered their usual [SELECT id=38], giving shoppers more reason than ever to use programs that seemed outdated just ten years ago. [CITE source=Focus, January 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1f Commentary: The Curious Capitalist**

​

If you haven’t viewed the video of hedge-fund billionaire Paul Tudor Jones telling a symposium at the University of Virginia how women lose their ability to be good financial traders after giving birth, go watch it now; it must be seen to be believed. As Jones puts it, “You’ll never see as many great women investors or traders as men” because the hugely emotional experience of having kids is simply too distracting. To be fair, he also says he notices a 10% to 20% performance drop in employees of either gender when they are going through the turmoil of divorce. But the point in either case seems to be that good traders shouldn’t be at the mercy of their emotions or biology.

​

That’s actually true over the long haul, which is what makes Jones’ views about women so fascinating and so flawed. Evidence is growing that women do indeed approach trading differently from men. In short: if you look at longer-term returns, women are just as good if not better. What’s more, understanding the mechanisms that apparently drive the gender differences could help us develop rules to make our markets safer. It all stems from research into the relationship between emotions and trading. Some of the most interesting studies have been done by a former Goldman Sachs and Deutsche Bank trader turned neuroscientist, John Coates, whose book The Hour between Dog and Wolf lays out the biochemistry of the Big Trade. 

​

When Coates switched from finance to neurological research (he’s now a fellow at the University of Cambridge), he was stunned to learn about a phenomenon that biologists call the winner effect. Animals that win one fight are more likely to win another, as the winner enjoys higher testosterone levels, which provide an edge in subsequent battles. It’s a concept at odds with the notion in financial circles that there is no such thing as a hot hand – a winning streak that becomes a self-fulfilling snowball of profits. In this view, a trader’s success simply reflects statistical probabilities bolstered by the time and effort spent trading, not by anything chemical. Financial economists prefer to see their dismal profession as a cool, clean discipline à la physics rather than a messy pseudoscience dependent on humans. 

​

Intrigued, Coates began running tests on a group of high-frequency male traders in London – and found what he had long suspected. Their morning levels of testosterone predicted their afternoon returns. The higher their T levels, the more money they made. Over the short term, that is. The higher T levels and the cocktail of other risk-encouraging hormones that went along with them pushed the traders to become excessive risk takers and resulted in major losses.

​

All this helps shed light on some observations about gender and finance. The biggest winners and losers tend to be male. Female traders, meanwhile, often do better in the long term by avoiding the highs and lows. In a study of more than 35,000 traders from 1991 to 1997 women outperformed men in large part because they didn’t trade as often. A 2009 study in which female traders trumped men over a nine-year-period had similar findings.

​

The research has fascinating policy implications for firms and even governments. Certain hedge funds have begun automatically pulling traders off their desks when their losses go beyond 3% of capital, which makes them vulnerable to enhanced risk taking in efforts to make up their losses. As markets begin to think long-term, I hope we see a kind of natural affirmative action effect: women and older workers (who are also less likely to go on testosterone-fueled trading binges) will start to look better and better. And if the billionaire Paul Tudor Jones is lucky, a few of them may still be willing to work for him. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

content.text:
**1.1f Commentary: The Curious Capitalist**

​

If you haven’t viewed the video of hedge-fund billionaire Paul Tudor Jones telling a symposium at the University of Virginia how women lose their ability to be good financial traders after giving birth, go watch it now; it must be seen to be believed. As Jones puts it, “You’ll never see as many great women investors or traders as men” because the hugely emotional experience of having kids is simply too distracting. To be fair, he also says he notices a 10% to 20% performance drop in employees of either gender when they are going through the turmoil of divorce. But the point in either case seems to be that good traders shouldn’t be at the mercy of their emotions or biology.

​

That’s actually true over the long haul, which is what makes Jones’ views about women so fascinating and so flawed. Evidence is growing that women do indeed approach trading differently from men. In short: if you look at longer-term returns, women are just as good if not better. What’s more, understanding the mechanisms that apparently drive the gender differences could help us develop rules to make our markets safer. It all stems from research into the relationship between emotions and trading. Some of the most interesting studies have been done by a former Goldman Sachs and Deutsche Bank trader turned neuroscientist, John Coates, whose book The Hour between Dog and Wolf lays out the biochemistry of the Big Trade. 

​

When Coates switched from finance to neurological research (he’s now a fellow at the University of Cambridge), he was stunned to learn about a phenomenon that biologists call the winner effect. Animals that win one fight are more likely to win another, as the winner enjoys higher testosterone levels, which provide an edge in subsequent battles. It’s a concept at odds with the notion in financial circles that there is no such thing as a hot hand – a winning streak that becomes a self-fulfilling snowball of profits. In this view, a trader’s success simply reflects statistical probabilities bolstered by the time and effort spent trading, not by anything chemical. Financial economists prefer to see their dismal profession as a cool, clean discipline à la physics rather than a messy pseudoscience dependent on humans.

Female traders, meanwhile, often do better in the long term by avoiding the highs and lows. A 2009 study in which female traders trumped men over a nine-year-period had similar findings.

The research has fascinating policy implications for firms and even governments. Certain hedge funds have begun automatically pulling traders off their desks when their losses go beyond 3% of capital, which makes them vulnerable to enhanced risk taking in efforts to make up their losses. As markets begin to think long-term, I hope we see a kind of natural affirmative action effect: women and older workers (who are also less likely to go on testosterone-fueled trading binges) will start to look better and better. And if the billionaire Paul Tudor Jones is lucky, a few of them may still be willing to work for him. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1c Spotlight: **_**Magicicada**_

​

After seventeen years in the soil, an insect called Magicicada reappears to mate again. Periodical cicadas spend over a decade buried beneath the soil, feeding on plant xylem. And then one spring, they emerge, shed, sing, mate and die in a few short weeks. Male cicadas gather in large groups to produce a mating song that can be deafening. The sound is the result of the buckling and contracting of the tymbal – a membrane that resonates in its stomach. An interested female Magicicada responds with a click of her wing. Periodical cicadas tunnel up during spring and emerge around May. There are three 17- year cicada species and four of the 13-year kind. Female cicadas can lay up to 400 eggs which fall from the trees onto the ground and emerge when the soil is 18 degrees Celcius. The reason behind their cycle of emergence still remains unknown. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

content.text:
**1.1c Spotlight: **_**Magicicada**_

​

After seventeen years in the soil, an insect called Magicicada reappears to mate again. Periodical cicadas spend over a decade buried beneath the soil, feeding on plant xylem. And then one spring, they emerge, shed, sing, mate and die in a few short weeks. Male cicadas gather in large groups to produce a mating song that can be deafening. The sound is the result of the buckling and contracting of the tymbal – a membrane that resonates in its stomach. An interested female Magicicada responds with a click of her wing. Periodical cicadas tunnel up during spring and emerge around May. There are three 17- year cicada species and four of the 13-year kind. Female cicadas can lay up to 400 eggs which fall from the trees onto the ground and emerge when the soil is 18 degrees Celcius. The reason behind their cycle of emergence still remains unknown. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1g Ladies Last**

​

How long do you have? It depends on gender and geography. In the U.S., women live longer – 81 years on average, 76 for men – but a recent study by the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation reveals a troubling phenomenon. Though men’s life spans have increased by 4.6 years since 1989, women have gained only 2.7 years. “This is a wake-up call,” says study co-author Ali Mokdad. Geographic gaps have also widened; parts of the South are a full decade behind places like coastal California. Public health initiatives like drinking bans and more sidewalks allowing people to go for a stroll have made a big difference, says Mokdad. “People are living to 86, on average, in some parts of the world. Why shouldn’t Americans do the same?” [CITE source=National Geographic, April 2013]

content.text:
**1.1g Ladies Last**

​

How long do you have? It depends on gender and geography. In the U.S., women live longer – 81 years on average, 76 for men – but a recent study by the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation reveals a troubling phenomenon. Though men’s life spans have increased by 4.6 years since 1989, women have gained only 2.7 years. “This is a wake-up call,” says study co-author Ali Mokdad. Geographic gaps have also widened; parts of the South are a full decade behind places like coastal California. Public health initiatives like drinking bans and more sidewalks allowing people to go for a stroll have made a big difference, says Mokdad. “People are living to 86, on average, in some parts of the world. Why shouldn’t Americans do the same?” [CITE source=National Geographic, April 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1e Upfront Ideas: Finding Your Element**

​

_In the following the author of Finding Your Element, Ken Robinson, discusses the notion of one’s ‘element’. _

The ‘element’ is the point where natural talents meet personal passions. When we’re in our element, we feel at our most authentic; that we’re doing what we’re meant to be doing in our lives. Finding your element is a two-way journey. It’s an inner journey to discover more about yourself, your own aptitudes and what excites you. It’s also an outward journey, to explore new opportunities and aspects of yourself that you may never have focused on before. Many people feel that they have no sense of purpose or meaning and little sense of direction. You can see it in the rising rates of depression and disengagement at work. Finding your element is essential to knowing more about who you really are. We all have deep talents and possibilities, but human resources are like other natural resources – they’re often buried beneath the surface and have to be discovered and refined. The element may be different for each of us, but finding it is vital for all of us. [CITE source=Psychologies Magazine, June 2013]

content.text:
**1.1e Upfront Ideas: Finding Your Element**

​

_In the following the author of Finding Your Element, Ken Robinson, discusses the notion of one’s ‘element’. _

The ‘element’ is the point where natural talents meet personal passions. When we’re in our element, we feel at our most authentic; that we’re doing what we’re meant to be doing in our lives. Finding your element is a two-way journey. It’s an inner journey to discover more about yourself, your own aptitudes and what excites you. It’s also an outward journey, to explore new opportunities and aspects of yourself that you may never have focused on before. Many people feel that they have no sense of purpose or meaning and little sense of direction. You can see it in the rising rates of depression and disengagement at work. Finding your element is essential to knowing more about who you really are. We all have deep talents and possibilities, but human resources are like other natural resources – they’re often buried beneath the surface and have to be discovered and refined. The element may be different for each of us, but finding it is vital for all of us. [CITE source=Psychologies Magazine, June 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1b Heart and Soles**

​

While volunteering at a Philadelphia soup kitchen in 2007, Tom Costello Jr. learned that many of the foot-related afflictions from which homeless people suffer – swelling, blisters, skin infections, even frostbite – can be avoided by wearing new, dry socks. But because socks generally aren’t included in charitable clothing donations, most people in need go without them. “I knew I had to be the sock guy,” he says. 

​

So Costello founded the Joy of Sox, which distributes donated socks to shelters across the country. He gets the word out through social media, local sock drives, and a mascot, Joy the Sock Puppet, who accompanies Costello on speaking trips. He even persuaded the Pennsylvania State Senate to recognize February 14 as Pennsylvania Socks for the Homeless Day. It’s a small victory for the more than 14,000 homeless in Pennsylvania, but Costello isn’t daunted. “I once ran a marathon,” he says, “and someone asked me how I did it. I said, ‘One block at a time.’ You do it little by little.” [CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, December 2012]

content.text:
**1.1b Heart and Soles**

​

While volunteering at a Philadelphia soup kitchen in 2007, Tom Costello Jr. learned that many of the foot-related afflictions from which homeless people suffer – swelling, blisters, skin infections, even frostbite – can be avoided by wearing new, dry socks. But because socks generally aren’t included in charitable clothing donations, most people in need go without them. “I knew I had to be the sock guy,” he says. 

​

So Costello founded the Joy of Sox, which distributes donated socks to shelters across the country. He gets the word out through social media, local sock drives, and a mascot, Joy the Sock Puppet, who accompanies Costello on speaking trips. He even persuaded the Pennsylvania State Senate to recognize February 14 as Pennsylvania Socks for the Homeless Day. It’s a small victory for the more than 14,000 homeless in Pennsylvania, but Costello isn’t daunted. “I once ran a marathon,” he says, “and someone asked me how I did it. I said, ‘One block at a time.’ You do it little by little.” [CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, December 2012]

questions.main_text:
**The greatest achievements of the Maya **

By following one of the several Maya calendars, the so-called Long Court calendar, for which a full cycle lasted 5,126 of our years, experts have pinpointed the end of the Long Count circle to 21 December 2012. As to whether the Maya really predicted an apocalypse to take place in 2012, Mayanists are fairly unanimous in answering with a resounding “no”. Professor David Stuart is unequivocal: “No authentic Maya text foretells the end of the world in 2012, or of any destructive event happening in connection with the turn of the thirteenth sub-period in the Long Count.” Norman Hammond believes past evidence counts against a doomsday in 2012. “When they reached the end of earlier sub-periods, first in AD 41, and again in 435, there was no big deal, only a few monuments put up.” 

​

Even if the Maya might not have predicted the end of the world, the ancient Maya civilization continues to fascinate scholars and the public at large, for good reason. Some of the architecture is breathtaking and the Maya hieroglyphs are capable of expressing great subtleties of language. It is also true that despite the confusion it has led to, it’s the complex Maya calendar system that fascinates us with all its cycles and sub-periods. Furthermore, the fact that the Maya were intertwined with both the past and the future in their calendars is also truly remarkable. “Time for them was like playing a pinball machine,” says Hammond. Today, we don’t have calculations like that. [CITE source=BBC History Magazine, Christmas 2012]

content.text:
**The greatest achievements of the Maya **

By following one of the several Maya calendars, the so-called Long Court calendar, for which a full cycle lasted 5,126 of our years, experts have pinpointed the end of the Long Count circle to 21 December 2012. As to whether the Maya really predicted an apocalypse to take place in 2012, Mayanists are fairly unanimous in answering with a resounding “no”. Professor David Stuart is unequivocal: “No authentic Maya text foretells the end of the world in 2012, or of any destructive event happening in connection with the turn of the thirteenth sub-period in the Long Count.” Norman Hammond believes past evidence counts against a doomsday in 2012. “When they reached the end of earlier sub-periods, first in AD 41, and again in 435, there was no big deal, only a few monuments put up.” 

​

Even if the Maya might not have predicted the end of the world, the ancient Maya civilization continues to fascinate scholars and the public at large, for good reason. Some of the architecture is breathtaking and the Maya hieroglyphs are capable of expressing great subtleties of language. It is also true that despite the confusion it has led to, it’s the complex Maya calendar system that fascinates us with all its cycles and sub-periods. Furthermore, the fact that the Maya were intertwined with both the past and the future in their calendars is also truly remarkable. “Time for them was like playing a pinball machine,” says Hammond. Today, we don’t have calculations like that. [CITE source=BBC History Magazine, Christmas 2012]

questions.main_text:
**1.1d Scatology**

​

To conservation biologists like Samuel Wasser, an animal’s droppings are hardly waste. Each pellet or patty packs a history of an animal’s diet, territory, and sexual state. Most mammals can sniff out this info; human trackers – with less keen noses – have had to rely on other methods. Enter Wasser. His University of Washington lab has pioneered more advanced means of studying scat, including DNA extraction. That means less need for dart guns. “Once we had DNA,” he says, “we could identify an animal without seeing it. Essentially, we could connect the dots.” 

​

For the past decade Wasser has been connecting elephant “dots” across Africa. He collects the animals’ dung, classifies the samples, and adds the information to a growing reference map. By matching DNA from seized tusks with DNA from dung sites he can even pinpoint ivory-poaching hot spots. But the map has gaps. In the heart of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where few elephants remain, Wasser had little data. So in March 2011 he enlisted two National Geographic grantees, Trip Jennings and Andy Maser, for what Maser calls “CSI: Elephant.” The duo found fresh samples. More important, they taught locals how to collect dung and send it directly to Wasser’s lab. Now Wasser is picking through their finds. With each new shipment, he gathers hope for elephants – in heaps. [CITE source=National Geographic, January 2013]

content.text:
**1.1d Scatology**

​

To conservation biologists like Samuel Wasser, an animal’s droppings are hardly waste. Each pellet or patty packs a history of an animal’s diet, territory, and sexual state. Most mammals can sniff out this info; human trackers – with less keen noses – have had to rely on other methods. Enter Wasser. His University of Washington lab has pioneered more advanced means of studying scat, including DNA extraction. That means less need for dart guns. “Once we had DNA,” he says, “we could identify an animal without seeing it. Essentially, we could connect the dots.” 

​

For the past decade Wasser has been connecting elephant “dots” across Africa. He collects the animals’ dung, classifies the samples, and adds the information to a growing reference map. By matching DNA from seized tusks with DNA from dung sites he can even pinpoint ivory-poaching hot spots. But the map has gaps. In the heart of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where few elephants remain, Wasser had little data. So in March 2011 he enlisted two National Geographic grantees, Trip Jennings and Andy Maser, for what Maser calls “CSI: Elephant.” The duo found fresh samples. More important, they taught locals how to collect dung and send it directly to Wasser’s lab. Now Wasser is picking through their finds. With each new shipment, he gathers hope for elephants – in heaps. [CITE source=National Geographic, January 2013]

questions.main_text:
**Understanding the other gender** 

Participate in this discussion on Young People’s Discussion Forum on Facebook. Write your own entry. 

​

_It’s been more than 23 years, but I still haven’t understood “Girls”. In my life I have interacted with a lot of girls but each of them had a different philosophy of life and thinking. I always used to think about what was going on in her mind, but whatever I thought turned out to be wrong. – Desperate – _

​

_\*\*\* _

​

_Dear Desperate! I can so relate to what you’re saying! But, I’m a girl. And believe it or not, I can’t understand guys. Why don’t they talk about anything??? And what is this guy bonding thing? – Clueless –_

​

content.text:
**Understanding the other gender** 

Participate in this discussion on Young People’s Discussion Forum on Facebook. Write your own entry. 

​

_It’s been more than 23 years, but I still haven’t understood “Girls”. In my life I have interacted with a lot of girls but each of them had a different philosophy of life and thinking. I always used to think about what was going on in her mind, but whatever I thought turned out to be wrong. – Desperate – _

​

_\*\*\* _

​

_Dear Desperate! I can so relate to what you’re saying! But, I’m a girl. And believe it or not, I can’t understand guys. Why don’t they talk about anything??? And what is this guy bonding thing? – Clueless –_

​

questions.main_text:
**1.1a Young Millenials**

​

Re: “The New Greatest Generation” [May 20]: While the generation of young millennials, wired and generally active on the Web, may be moving ahead on their own, they are leaving a lot of their peers behind. Not all of them end up becoming celebrities or YouTube directors. Most become buyers, who just stay in the stands as spectators. Instagram, with just 13 employees, was sold to Facebook for $1 billion. Only a handful of people profited from the sale. How will the spectators sustain themselves? If nothing is done about the narcissism of millennials, they’ll implode. They should keep in mind that technology must lead to empowerment of the individual – and that includes both responsibility and respect. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

content.text:
**1.1a Young Millenials**

​

Re: “The New Greatest Generation” [May 20]: While the generation of young millennials, wired and generally active on the Web, may be moving ahead on their own, they are leaving a lot of their peers behind. Not all of them end up becoming celebrities or YouTube directors. Most become buyers, who just stay in the stands as spectators. Instagram, with just 13 employees, was sold to Facebook for $1 billion. Only a handful of people profited from the sale. How will the spectators sustain themselves? If nothing is done about the narcissism of millennials, they’ll implode. They should keep in mind that technology must lead to empowerment of the individual – and that includes both responsibility and respect. [CITE source=Time, June 2013]

questions.main_text:
**1.1h Letter Perfect**

​

“I’m not fond of writing letters,” Willa Cather confided to poet Edna St. Vincent Millay, but nearly seven decades after the celebrated novelist’s death, it’s clear the lady protested too much. Collected for the first time, Cather’s prolific correspondence traces the evolution of her craft, from her youth on the Nebraska prairie to her sorrowdrenched decline in New York. In that pre-texting, pre-Twitter age, she kept the postal service busy. Cather moved among the eminent literary circles of her day, corresponding with F. Scott Fitzgerald, Sinclair Lewis, and Langston Hughes – but the personal details of her life she reserved only for the closest ones in the family. In _The Selected Letters of Willa Cather_, both sides come to life. Virtually every letter contains some insight about writing, a hammer or chisel for her toolbox. “As one grows older one cares less about clever writing and more about a straight forward and faithful presentation,” she once confessed. “But to reach this, one must have gone through the period where one would die, so to speak, for the fine phrase.” In her last years, Cather withdrew into a cone of morose isolation, plagued by ill health and the horrors of World War II. “I have cared too much, about people and places – cared too hard,” she wrote to her brother. “It made me, as a writer. But it will break me in the end.” [CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, May 2013]

content.text:
**1.1h Letter Perfect**

​

“I’m not fond of writing letters,” Willa Cather confided to poet Edna St. Vincent Millay, but nearly seven decades after the celebrated novelist’s death, it’s clear the lady protested too much. Collected for the first time, Cather’s prolific correspondence traces the evolution of her craft, from her youth on the Nebraska prairie to her sorrowdrenched decline in New York. In that pre-texting, pre-Twitter age, she kept the postal service busy. Cather moved among the eminent literary circles of her day, corresponding with F. Scott Fitzgerald, Sinclair Lewis, and Langston Hughes – but the personal details of her life she reserved only for the closest ones in the family. In _The Selected Letters of Willa Cather_, both sides come to life. Virtually every letter contains some insight about writing, a hammer or chisel for her toolbox. “As one grows older one cares less about clever writing and more about a straight forward and faithful presentation,” she once confessed. “But to reach this, one must have gone through the period where one would die, so to speak, for the fine phrase.” In her last years, Cather withdrew into a cone of morose isolation, plagued by ill health and the horrors of World War II. “I have cared too much, about people and places – cared too hard,” she wrote to her brother. “It made me, as a writer. But it will break me in the end.” [CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, May 2013]

questions.main_text:
**Birds’ sleep **

Birds cannot risk long, deep sleep because they constantly need to be alert to danger. Moreover, many species have long migration flights, with individual legs of migration sometimes exceeding 24 hours. Instead of allowing the whole brain to sleep, birds let each hemisphere of the brain and its associated eye take turns in resting, in a process called unilateral eye closure. This is the biological equivalent of autopilot – the active half of the brain can still alert them to danger. Between flights, migrating birds catch up with missed sleep by drifting into an intermediate sleep-like state referred to as drowsiness, when they keep a lookout through drooping eyelids. Although not as refreshing as normal sleep, this has the advantage of decreasing the risk of being caught and eaten. That’s the latest news about bird migration. – Amba Sanghera [CITE source=NewScientist, 15 December 2012]

content.text:
**Birds’ sleep **

Birds cannot risk long, deep sleep because they constantly need to be alert to danger. Moreover, many species have long migration flights, with individual legs of migration sometimes exceeding 24 hours. Instead of allowing the whole brain to sleep, birds let each hemisphere of the brain and its associated eye take turns in resting, in a process called unilateral eye closure. This is the biological equivalent of autopilot – the active half of the brain can still alert them to danger. Between flights, migrating birds catch up with missed sleep by drifting into an intermediate sleep-like state referred to as drowsiness, when they keep a lookout through drooping eyelids. Although not as refreshing as normal sleep, this has the advantage of decreasing the risk of being caught and eaten. That’s the latest news about bird migration. – Amba Sanghera [CITE source=NewScientist, 15 December 2012]

questions.main_text:
**The cause of mass murder **

Around 251 million years ago, over 90 per cent of the species on Earth suddenly went extinct. The prevailing theory is that the mass extinction was triggered by volcanic eruptions over a vast area of what is now Siberia. But according to Daniel Rothman of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, atmospheric carbon levels surged much too quickly for geological processes to be at work. Microbes can produce carbon compounds that fast, though. When Rothman’s group analyzed the microbe that is responsible for most of the Earth’s methane today, they discovered that the microbe gained this ability at about the time of the mass extinction. However, this microbe needs large amounts of nickel to produce greenhouse gas. The researchers discovered that nickel levels rose suddenly almost exactly 251 million years ago – probably because the Siberian lavas were rich in the metal. This process utilizing nickel, Rothman claims, triggered the extinction. Other geologists remain to be convinced. – Sarah Reardon [CITE source=NewScientist, 15 December 2012]

content.text:
**The cause of mass murder **

Around 251 million years ago, over 90 per cent of the species on Earth suddenly went extinct. The prevailing theory is that the mass extinction was triggered by volcanic eruptions over a vast area of what is now Siberia. But according to Daniel Rothman of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, atmospheric carbon levels surged much too quickly for geological processes to be at work. Microbes can produce carbon compounds that fast, though. When Rothman’s group analyzed the microbe that is responsible for most of the Earth’s methane today, they discovered that the microbe gained this ability at about the time of the mass extinction. However, this microbe needs large amounts of nickel to produce greenhouse gas. The researchers discovered that nickel levels rose suddenly almost exactly 251 million years ago – probably because the Siberian lavas were rich in the metal. This process utilizing nickel, Rothman claims, triggered the extinction. Other geologists remain to be convinced. – Sarah Reardon [CITE source=NewScientist, 15 December 2012]

questions.main_text:
**Mars: a bit like Hawaii**

Patches of soil on the Red Planet show a [SELECT id=14] to the volcanic soils that are found in Hawaii, an analysis [SELECT id=15] by NASA’s Mars rover Curiosity has revealed. Instruments installed on the car-sized vehicle are allowing Martian soil to be analyzed in [SELECT id=16] detail. Because the mixture of minerals present reflects the conditions under which they formed, studying it can offer new [SELECT id=17] into Mars’s past – and an indication [SELECT id=18] conditions were ever suitable for life to appear.

​

A recent sample collected by Curiosity from the Gale Crater contains minerals just like the soils on Hawaii. Unlike the several billion-year-old rock first sampled by Curiosity, which indicated the presence of flowing water in the past, this sample reflects more modern processes and shows [SELECT id=19] sign of water having been present.

​

Curiosity’s Chemistry and Mineralogy (CheMin) instrument was used to analyze the sample. It uses X-ray diffraction, in which a mineral’s internal structure is [SELECT id=20] how its crystals interact with X-rays. The mineralogy of this patch of sand [SELECT id=21] by researchers even before the analysis was made. But, in their opinion, things should get particularly interesting when the rover reaches its ultimate destination within the Gale Crater, the 5km-high Mount Sharp.

– Kelly Oakes [CITE source=Focus, January 2013]

questions.main_text:
Shopping with the Romans

If you fancied some serious retail therapy in the ancient world then the place to be was Rome, [GAP id=1] streets drew in people and produce from across [GAP id=2] and beyond. [GAP id=3] BC, as many as one million inhabitants lived in Rome, which is an urban population figure [GAP id=4] again in the western world until London in around 1800.

​

Like most urban residents, the people of Rome relied on retailers to provide them with food, clothing and other goods. Our ancient evidence points to a thriving retail trade in the city, and for [GAP id=5] ancient visitor the sheer number of retailers and shoppers [GAP id=6] been a particularly striking aspect of the Roman cityscape. Market traders and street sellers [GAP id=7] cluster around temples, bathhouses, forums and circuses, attracted by the commercial opportunities among [GAP id=8].

​

Ancient paintings show tableaus of everyday events in the forum. Along with people reading public notices, we can make out traders hawking their wares. Some have their goods [GAP id=9] around them, whereas bread and vegetable vendors often display their wares on wooden tables or in baskets [GAP id=10]. Ancient literary sources almost universally complain about retailers being unscrupulous, even deceitful, in their urge to compete for [GAP id=11] money. [GAP id=12], one of the authors remarked that it was not uncommon that sometimes wealthy residents [GAP id=13] by retailers in their homes, to cater for them. All in all, the retail trade was an essential sector of the urban economy in ancient Rome. [CITE source=BBC History Magazine, Christmas 2012]
